section.title: Greeting and the Gospel of God
section.sectionRationale: Paul opens the greatest letter of the New Testament not with himself but with the gospel. In one long, densely packed sentence he introduces himself as a servant and apostle, then defines the gospel: it is God's gospel, promised beforehand in the Scriptures, concerning His Son, who is both David's descendant according to the flesh and the Son of God in power by the resurrection. The greeting itself is a compressed statement of the whole letter to come.
section.thesis: The gospel is God's own good news, long promised and now fulfilled in His Son Jesus Christ, the risen Lord; and this gospel creates a people, called and beloved, set apart for the obedience of faith among all the nations.
section.frameNote: Three movements within the greeting: Paul the servant set apart for the gospel; the gospel defined as the good news of God's Son, descended from David and declared Son of God by resurrection; and the recipients, called and loved saints in Rome.

units.label: Paul, a servant set apart for the gospel

verses.text: Paul, a {{doulos|servant}} of Christ Jesus, called to be an {{apostolos|apostle}}, and set apart for the {{euangelion|gospel}} of God:

units.label: The gospel concerning His Son
units.note: descended from David, declared Son of God by resurrection

verses.text: the gospel He promised beforehand through His prophets in the Holy Scriptures,
regarding His Son, who was a descendant of David according to the flesh,
and who through the Spirit of holiness was declared with power to be the Son of God by His resurrection from the dead: Jesus Christ our Lord.

units.label: Called and loved saints in Rome
units.note: the obedience of faith among the nations

verses.text: Through Him and on behalf of His name, we received grace and apostleship to call all those among the Gentiles to the {{hypakoe|obedience}} that comes from faith.
And you also are among those who are called to belong to Jesus Christ.
To all in Rome who are loved by God and called to be {{hagioi|saints}}: Grace and peace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

doulos.gloss: A slave or bondservant, one wholly owned by and devoted to another. Paul opens by naming himself a slave of Christ Jesus, a title of both humility and honor, echoing the servants of the LORD in the Old Testament like Moses and the prophets. His whole life is now owned by his Master.

apostolos.gloss: One sent with authority as a commissioned representative. Paul is called to be an apostle, not self-appointed but summoned by the risen Christ and set apart for the gospel. The word carries the weight of the sender: to receive the apostle's message is to receive Christ.

euangelion.gloss: Good news, glad tidings, announcement of victory. It is the gospel of God, both from God as its source and about what God has done in His Son. In the ancient world the word announced a royal birth or a triumph; here it announces the greater triumph of God in Christ crucified and risen.

hypakoe.gloss: Obedience, a hearing that submits. The obedience of faith is the response the gospel calls for among all nations: faith that trusts and therefore obeys. It frames the whole letter, which opens and closes with the obedience that comes from faith.

hagioi.gloss: Holy ones, saints, those set apart for God. It is not a title for the elite few but for all believers, the whole church in Rome, made holy in Christ and called to live as God's own people.

basic.context: This is the opening of Paul's letter to the church in Rome, a church he did not found and had not yet visited. In the ancient world a letter began by naming the sender, the recipients, and a greeting. Paul takes that simple form and fills it with the gospel. Before he says anything about himself, he tells us whose he is and what he has been sent to announce: the good news of God concerning His Son.
basic.hermeneutics: These seven verses are one long sentence in Greek, so read them as a single thought that circles around the gospel. Verses 3 and 4 may be an early confession of faith Paul quotes, a summary the first Christians already sang or recited. The greeting is not throat-clearing; it plants the themes of the whole letter: gospel, Scripture fulfilled, Christ's descent and resurrection, faith, obedience, and the nations.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul is a doulos, a slave of Christ, and an apostolos, one sent with authority. The message is the euangelion, the good news of God. He aims at the hypakoe of faith, the obedience that flows from trust, and he writes to the hagioi, the saints, all who belong to Christ.
basic.typology: Jesus as the descendant of David fulfills the promise to David of a son whose throne would last forever. The prophets who promised the gospel beforehand point to the One in whom every promise finds its yes. Paul as a set-apart servant stands in the line of Moses and the prophets, servants of the LORD, now serving the risen Lord.
basic.christ: The gospel is entirely about Jesus. He is God's Son, truly human as David's descendant, and declared Son of God in power by rising from the dead. He is Jesus Christ our Lord, the crucified and risen King. Grace and apostleship, and our very calling, all come through Him and for His name.
basic.god: This is the gospel of God: He is its author and its source. He promised it long ago through His prophets, He sent His Son, He raised Him from the dead, and He calls and loves a people for Himself. Grace and peace flow from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 7 promises David a son whose kingdom endures. Habakkuk 2:4, the righteous will live by faith, sounds through the whole letter. Acts 9 records Paul's calling by the risen Christ. Romans 16:26 closes the letter as it opens, with the obedience of faith among all nations.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible had been promising a rescuer. Here Paul announces that the promise is kept: the good news of God, foretold by the prophets, has arrived in His Son, the son of David and Son of God, raised from the dead. The story that ran from Eden through David now opens out to all the nations, gathering a beloved, called people.

context.literary: The opening sentence of the letter, verses 1 to 7, is a single, carefully built greeting: sender, gospel summary, recipients, blessing. Its center of gravity is the gospel, and especially verses 3 and 4, which appear to preserve an early creedal formula about the Son. Everything that unfolds over sixteen chapters is compressed here in seed form.
context.historical: Paul writes around AD 57, likely from Corinth, to a mixed church of Jewish and Gentile believers in the imperial capital. He had never been to Rome but planned to visit on his way to Spain. Introducing himself carefully to a church he did not plant, he leads with his God-given commission and the gospel they hold in common.
context.canonical: Romans is the fullest statement of the gospel in the New Testament, and its greeting sets the agenda. The confession of verses 3 and 4 joins the son of David of the promises to the risen Son of God, holding together the two Testaments. The obedience of faith brackets the letter, first here and last in the closing doxology.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To introduce himself to a church he had not yet visited, to establish his God-given authority as an apostle, and above all to announce the gospel of God concerning His Son, so that the Roman believers would recognize the message they share and be prepared for the argument to come.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The greeting describes Paul's calling and the content of the gospel. Its abiding truth is prescriptive for the church in every age: the gospel is God's, centered on His crucified and risen Son, and it calls all peoples to the obedience that comes from faith.

debates.question: Are verses 3 and 4 an early Christian confession Paul is quoting?

views.label: A pre-Pauline creed
views.case: The balanced two-line structure, distinct vocabulary, and confessional feel suggest Paul is drawing on a formula already known to the churches, affirming common ground with Rome.

views.label: Paul's own fresh composition
views.case: The lines fit Paul's theology so well that he may simply be writing his own summary; either way the content is fully his.

debates.bearing: Whether quoted or composed, the confession expresses the shared apostolic gospel: Jesus is David's son and the risen Son of God, and Paul stands on that common ground with the Roman church.

debates.question: What does declared with power to be the Son of God by His resurrection mean?

views.label: Publicly appointed and displayed
views.case: The eternal Son, already the Son, was openly appointed the Son of God in power at the resurrection, entering a new stage of His mission as the exalted, life-giving Lord.

views.label: Adoptionist misreading
views.case: Some have wrongly taken it to mean Jesus became the Son only at the resurrection; this contradicts verse 3, which already calls Him His Son before speaking of His human descent.

debates.bearing: The historic reading guards Christ's eternal sonship: He was always the Son, and by the resurrection He was powerfully declared and installed as the reigning Son of God, our Lord.

study.originalLanguages: Paul introduces himself with three words that set the tone: he is a doulos, a slave owned by Christ; called to be an apostolos, sent with Christ's own authority; and set apart for the euangelion, the good news of God. The goal of that gospel is the hypakoe of faith, an obedience that is the fruit of trust, sought among all the nations. And the readers are the hagioi, the holy ones, all who belong to Jesus Christ and are loved by God.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, David's descendant according to the flesh, the promised King whose throne endures forever, 2 Samuel 7:12-16.
Paul as a set-apart slave of Christ stands in the line of Moses and the prophets who served the LORD, now serving the risen Lord and His gospel.
The prophetic promises reach their yes in Christ, so that the Holy Scriptures and the good news are one continuous story.

study.christ: Every line bends toward Christ. He is God's Son, the theme of the gospel. He is truly human, a descendant of David according to the flesh, and He is the Son of God declared in power by His resurrection from the dead. He is Jesus Christ our Lord, and it is through Him that grace and apostleship come, on behalf of His name, calling the nations to faith. The greeting shows the gospel is not a program or a philosophy but a Person, the crucified and risen King.
study.god: The gospel is called the gospel of God: He is its source, its promiser, and its accomplisher. He spoke it beforehand through His prophets, He sent and raised His Son, and He now calls and loves a people, granting them grace and peace. The passage is thoroughly God-centered, presenting a God who keeps His ancient word and gathers a family from every nation for the honor of His Son's name.

crossReferences.note: The promise of a son of David whose kingdom will be established forever, fulfilled in Christ.
The righteous will live by faith, the text that anchors the letter's theme of righteousness by faith.
The risen Christ sets Paul apart as a chosen instrument to carry His name to the Gentiles.
The closing doxology echoes the opening: the gospel long hidden, now revealed, bringing the obedience of faith to all nations.

study.oneStory: From Eden onward God had promised a rescuer, and to David He promised a son whose reign would never end. The prophets kept announcing this good news beforehand. Now Paul declares that the promise is kept in Jesus, David's son and the risen Son of God, Lord of all. The old story of Israel opens out to embrace the nations, gathering a beloved people who answer the gospel with the obedience of faith.

quiz.q: Whose gospel is it, according to Paul's opening?

options.text: It is the gospel of God, from Him and about what He has done in His Son.
options.note: Paul calls it the gospel of God, promised by Him and centered on His Son.

options.text: It is the gospel Paul was entrusted to carry, which he calls my gospel.
options.note: Paul does call it my gospel elsewhere, but only as its herald; its source is God.

options.text: It is the gospel of the Roman church that they taught Paul.
options.note: The Roman believers receive the gospel; they are not its author or Paul's teachers.

options.text: It is a message with no clear source.
options.note: Paul is explicit: it is the gospel of God, long promised through His prophets.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe Jesus in verses 3 and 4?

options.text: David's descendant according to the flesh and the Son of God declared in power by His resurrection.
options.note: The confession holds together Christ's true humanity and His divine sonship.

options.text: David's descendant who became God's Son only at His resurrection.
options.note: He truly is David's son and was declared Son in power at the resurrection, but verse 3 already calls Him God's Son beforehand.

options.text: Only a human teacher descended from David.
options.note: He is David's son, but also the eternal Son of God declared in power.

options.text: A spirit being who only seemed to be human.
options.note: He is truly a descendant of David according to the flesh.

quiz.q: What response does the gospel aim to produce among the nations?

options.text: The obedience that comes from faith.
options.note: Paul names the obedience of faith as the goal, bracketing the whole letter.

options.text: A trust in Christ that also bears fruit in obeying Him.
options.note: Close to the mark: faith and obedience belong together, though Paul's exact phrase is the obedience of faith.

options.text: Obedience to the law of Moses as the way of acceptance.
options.note: The obedience Paul means flows from faith, not from law keeping for acceptance.

options.text: Political submission to Rome.
options.note: Paul speaks of obedience to Christ through faith, not allegiance to the empire.

quiz.q: Who are the recipients Paul addresses?

options.text: All in Rome who are loved by God and called to be saints.
options.note: He writes to the whole believing church, the beloved and called saints.

options.text: All the believers in Rome, though he singles out only the leaders.
options.note: He writes to all the beloved saints; the leaders are included but not singled out over the rest.

options.text: Only Jewish believers in Rome.
options.note: The church was mixed, and his commission reaches the Gentiles too.

options.text: Unbelievers in the city at large.
options.note: He addresses those already loved by God and called to be saints.

quiz.q: What does Paul call himself at the very start?

options.text: A servant of Christ Jesus, called to be an apostle, set apart for the gospel.
options.note: He leads with his identity as Christ's slave and commissioned messenger.

options.text: A servant of Christ sent to carry a message he was given.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but Paul also names his specific calling as an apostle set apart for the gospel.

options.text: A teacher of his own religious ideas.
options.note: He is a slave of Christ carrying God's gospel, not his own ideas.

options.text: The founder of the Roman church.
options.note: Paul did not found the Roman church and had not yet visited it.

questions: Paul defines himself first as a servant of Christ, owned by his Master. How would seeing yourself as belonging wholly to Christ reshape your sense of identity and purpose?
The gospel was promised beforehand through the prophets. How does it strengthen your faith to know that God kept a promise made centuries earlier in the coming of His Son?
Jesus is both David's son and the Son of God declared in power by the resurrection. Why does it matter for your hope that the One you trust is both fully human and the risen Lord?
Paul aims at the obedience that comes from faith. Where in your life is God calling you to an obedience that flows out of trusting Him rather than trying to earn His favor?
You are addressed as loved by God and called to be a saint. How would resting in being beloved and set apart change the way you live this week?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered servant is doulos, a bondservant or slave; it carries both lowliness and the honor of the Old Testament servants of the LORD. The verse ends with a dash in some editions leading into verse 2; here it is rendered with a colon to keep the sense while avoiding the dash.

Declared with power to be the Son of God renders a verb meaning appointed or marked out; it does not mean Jesus became the Son at the resurrection, since verse 3 already calls Him God's Son, but that He was openly installed as the reigning Son of God in power.

The obedience that comes from faith renders a Greek phrase that can be read as the obedience which is faith or the obedience that springs from faith; either way faith and obedience belong together and frame the letter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 1:1-7. The dash after gospel of God in verse 1 was rendered as a colon to avoid an em dash while preserving the flow into verse 2. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
